caveats: a: No occupation mix specific to NAICS 112990 contract laboratory-animal producers was found.
a: BLS agricultural-worker duties cover many animal-production settings and underrepresent regulated laboratory quality work.
a: AI assistance to decision support is not equivalent to removal of accountable labor.
rho: No longitudinal AI implementation study for commercial laboratory-animal breeding was found.
rho: Customer and institutional validation requirements may vary by study type.
rho: Implementation is applied only to the exposed opportunity in a, not to all labor.
e: No measured firm share for laboratory-animal production or contract colony services within 112990 was found.
e: A vendor may combine animal sales, breeding services, testing, and other research services across multiple industry codes.
e: The frozen dataset provides no defensible LMM firm count for this activity.
s5: No observed control-transfer rate for eligible contract laboratory-animal producers was found.
s5: License or registration changes need not represent a sale of control.
s5: Large integrated vendors and nonprofit institutions are not representative of LMM owner succession.
q: Public vendor materials describe service scope but not contract economics or pass-through.
q: Pricing power differs between rare custom models, standard cohorts, academic customers, and large pharmaceutical buyers.
q: Reduced animal use is demand loss in d5 when caused by research substitution, but lower excess production within a contract can also reduce billable volume and retention.
d5: FDA-regulated drug development is only part of laboratory-animal demand.
d5: Mice and rats bred for research are not comprehensively captured in Animal Welfare Act reporting.
d5: New genetically engineered models could offset part of the reduction in routine toxicology animals.
o: Animal Welfare Act coverage excludes some commonly used research animals and does not define the commercial lens.
o: Operator requirement does not guarantee that the current independent firm survives; work can be insourced or consolidated.
o: The estimate applies only to quantity remaining after the d5 reduction.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat project fees for customer-directed colony establishment, breeding, husbandry, genotyping coordination, health-status control, and delivery of specified research cohorts, typically under a statement of work or dedicated-supply arrangement.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS code combines unrelated animal-production models ranging from pet and bird breeding to deer, worms, mixed farms, and laboratory animals. It is narrowed to customer-dedicated laboratory-animal breeding and colony production because that is the identifiable recurring outsourced-service model; combining it with consumer and commodity animal sellers would make one screen incoherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Companion-animal, bird, deer, llama, worm, and mixed-animal farms selling animals or animal products; ordinary laboratory-animal inventory sales without a recurring customer-dedicated production service; captive institutional vivaria, hobby breeders, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market laboratory-animal producers that repeatedly manage customer-dedicated breeding colonies or provide contract breeding and cohort-production services for external biomedical-research customers.

a.bridge: BLS describes agricultural animal breeders and farmworkers rather than a wage-weighted occupation mix for contract laboratory-animal colonies, while the laboratory-animal sources describe required records and customer-facing colony workflows without measuring AI exposure. I map pedigree and health records, breeding-plan support, scheduling, inventory reconciliation, reporting, customer updates, and anomaly triage to AI exposure, while leaving feeding, cage sanitation, animal examination, handling, veterinary judgment, and controlled procedures largely unexposed. This may overstate substitution where records are already automated or require human sign-off, and understate it where LMM operators have substantial project-management and quality staff.
caveats: No occupation mix specific to NAICS 112990 contract laboratory-animal producers was found.
BLS agricultural-worker duties cover many animal-production settings and underrepresent regulated laboratory quality work.
AI assistance to decision support is not equivalent to removal of accountable labor.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census from contract laboratory-animal producers, including technicians, colony managers, veterinarians, quality staff, project managers, and back office, matched to current LIMS automation and observed generative-AI task trials.
a.rationale: The service has a meaningful data and coordination layer because each animal's identity, genotype, health, lineage, breeding status, and disposition must be tracked, but daily husbandry and welfare observation remain physical and continuous. Existing colony portals and management tools mean part of the administrative opportunity is already automated and is excluded.

d5.bridge: FDA policy concerns animal use in preclinical drug-safety studies, not total constant-price demand for outsourced laboratory-animal breeding, and it does not isolate the LMM service population. I translate the agency's active, time-bound move toward in-vitro, computational, and human-derived alternatives into lower year-five animal-production quantity, while retaining demand from basic research, disease models, non-FDA work, and studies without validated alternatives. The proxy may overstate decline because the initial roadmap focuses on selected uses such as monoclonal antibodies and primates, or understate it if alternatives diffuse faster across research programs.
caveats: FDA-regulated drug development is only part of laboratory-animal demand.
Mice and rats bred for research are not comprehensively captured in Animal Welfare Act reporting.
New genetically engineered models could offset part of the reduction in routine toxicology animals.
d5.change_evidence: Species- and use-specific procurement volumes from NIH, pharmaceutical sponsors, CROs, and contract breeders through 2031, linked to FDA submissions using new approach methodologies and to outsourced versus in-house colony shares.
d5.rationale: The demand headwind is explicit and already under implementation: FDA is encouraging and qualifying alternatives intended to reduce or replace animal testing. Specialized models and customer-dedicated colonies remain useful, and vendor offerings indicate ongoing long-term demand, but a material five-year quantity decline is more plausible than broad growth for the current animal-based service basket.

caveats: No measured firm share for laboratory-animal production or contract colony services within 112990 was found.
A vendor may combine animal sales, breeding services, testing, and other research services across multiple industry codes.
The frozen dataset provides no defensible LMM firm count for this activity.
e.change_evidence: A business-level census linking 112990 establishments to APHIS license records, species, revenue by product versus contract breeding or colony management, customer recurrence, and normalized EBITDA sufficient to identify the LMM band.
e.rationale: Census defines 112990 around animal raising for market and lists multiple unrelated species and business models. Customer-dedicated laboratory breeding clearly exists, but it is only one illustrative activity and much laboratory-animal production remains product supply rather than recurring outsourced service. The qualifying share of LMM firms is therefore bounded as small.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($174,024) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

o.bridge: APHIS rules and inspection practices establish accountable human and organizational responsibility for covered animal care, records, veterinary programs, and handling, while the Guide describes trained personnel and an attending veterinarian; neither source directly measures the share of future service quantity requiring a lens operator. I infer that animal cohorts still demanded in year five will require an accountable breeding and husbandry organization even when software assists planning and monitoring. This may overstate independent-operator need if customers fully insource colonies or if uncovered species and facilities face different oversight.
caveats: Animal Welfare Act coverage excludes some commonly used research animals and does not define the commercial lens.
Operator requirement does not guarantee that the current independent firm survives; work can be insourced or consolidated.
The estimate applies only to quantity remaining after the d5 reduction.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customer insourcing, fully autonomous vivarium operation, regulator acceptance of materially reduced on-site staffing, and contract allocations showing who holds welfare, veterinary, genetic-integrity, and delivery accountability.
o.rationale: Software can optimize breeding, records, and monitoring but cannot itself hold licenses, provide continuous physical care, exercise veterinary authority, protect genetic and health status, or accept responsibility for animal welfare and delivery. Nearly all surviving demand should therefore retain an accountable operator, although some may migrate to captive vivaria or integrated platforms.

caveats: Public vendor materials describe service scope but not contract economics or pass-through.
Pricing power differs between rare custom models, standard cohorts, academic customers, and large pharmaceutical buyers.
Reduced animal use is demand loss in d5 when caused by research substitution, but lower excess production within a contract can also reduce billable volume and retention.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and renewal cohorts showing fee units, escalation clauses, open-book provisions, change orders, productivity guarantees, minimum volumes, term lengths, and realized repricing after documented process savings.
q.rationale: Custom colonies, complex genotypes, health status, reliability, and switching risk can protect some operator savings, especially during a live project. At renewal, sophisticated research customers can compare per-cage, per-animal, or milestone economics and demand lower fees, while efficiency that reduces excess breeding may directly lower billable volume. The base allows moderate retention but not permanent capture of the full implemented benefit.

caveats: No longitudinal AI implementation study for commercial laboratory-animal breeding was found.
Customer and institutional validation requirements may vary by study type.
Implementation is applied only to the exposed opportunity in a, not to all labor.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year deployment results from contract breeding vendors showing validated AI use cases, active-user rates, audit findings, false-negative rates for health or welfare exceptions, and realized labor hours avoided by workflow.
rho.rationale: Colony operations already use structured animal-level records and customer portals, making administrative AI technically plausible, but implementation must preserve traceability, validated procedures, animal welfare, data integrity, access controls, and human veterinary and quality oversight. The base assumes adoption in reporting, planning, scheduling, and exception prioritization, with limited autonomous action in husbandry or regulated records.

caveats: No observed control-transfer rate for eligible contract laboratory-animal producers was found.
License or registration changes need not represent a sale of control.
Large integrated vendors and nonprofit institutions are not representative of LMM owner succession.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction dataset from life-science M&A advisers and APHIS license-owner histories, matched to principal-owner ages, ownership changes, facility closures, customer-contract assignability, and normalized EBITDA.
s5.rationale: Qualifying firms are likely specialized, regulated, relationship-driven operations whose transfers depend on licenses, facilities, welfare record, customer consents, scientific staff, and colony continuity. These features can support strategic sales but also make ordinary retirement or asset liquidation less likely to become a transferable control deal. With no lens-specific owner-age or transaction series, the range is a bounded judgment.

principal_driver: Customer-dedicated colonies combine recurring operational complexity with data-rich planning, recordkeeping, and coordination workflows.
principal_weakness: The eligible service subset is tiny and unmeasured, while policy and technology are actively reducing the underlying use of research animals.

sources.supports: Defines the code as raising excluded-category or mixed animals for market and lists bird, companion animal, deer, laboratory animal, llama, and worm production as examples.
sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: 112990 All Other Animal Production

sources.supports: Describes animal-breeder recordkeeping and trait tracking alongside physical duties including feeding, cage cleaning, animal examination, medication, marking, and daily care.
sources.title: Agricultural Workers

sources.supports: Documents custom breeding, customer-dedicated supply colonies, continuous customer access to colony data, scaling, rederivation, cryopreservation, and ongoing or long-term project use.
sources.title: Mouse Breeding and Colony Management Services

sources.supports: Explains Animal Welfare Act licensing and registration, inspections of animals, records and facilities, periodic updates, and public access to licensee and research-facility records.
sources.title: USDA Animal Care Public Search Tool

sources.supports: States that USDA performs routine unannounced compliance inspections and reviews premises, records, husbandry, veterinary-care programs, and animal handling; covered research facilities are inspected at least annually.
sources.title: Animal Welfare Act Inspection and Annual Reports

sources.supports: Reports immediate implementation of a roadmap to reduce, refine, or replace animal testing with computational models, organoids, cell lines, and other new approach methodologies.
sources.title: FDA Announces Plan to Phase Out Animal Testing Requirement for Monoclonal Antibodies and Other Drugs

sources.supports: Reports 2026 progress implementing timeframes for phasing out animal testing, guidance reducing selected studies, expanded use of computational and in-vitro evidence, and qualification of an AI-based drug-development tool.
sources.title: FDA Achieves Year 1 Goals in Reducing Animal Testing in Drug Development

sources.supports: Describes institutional and veterinary accountability, qualified animal-care personnel, and the importance of demographic, clinical, pedigree, breeding, and research-use records.
sources.title: Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals, Eighth Edition

title: All Other Animal Production
